summary: Frontend-only regression testing completed for homepage scroll/camera behaviors in /app/refgd. Requested desktop/mobile scroll interactions passed in primary run, but an intermittent hydration warning was observed once in console.

integration_issues.issue: Intermittent React hydration mismatch error logged in browser console involving HomeCTAButton/EditableText subtree.

action_items: Fix intermittent hydration mismatch in Telegram CTA/HomeCTAButton region (server/client render divergence).
Re-run homepage console validation in dev and production build after hydration fix.
Optionally stabilize scroll lock sequencing between CosmicJourney and PathsHorizontalReveal listeners to reduce non-deterministic landing offsets under repeated hard wheel input.
critical_code_review_comments: CosmicJourney and PathsHorizontalReveal both attach document-level capture wheel/touch listeners; this can create race-like behavior under repeated large wheel deltas and make landing position non-deterministic.
Hydration mismatch stack points to HomeCTAButton/EditableText render output differing between SSR and client in at least one run.

success_rate.frontend: 90% (core requested scroll flows passed; console hydration check intermittently failed)

context_for_next_testing_agent: Primary automated run passed requested behaviors: desktop hard wheel landed with Path section visible + 5 cards visible + desktop flyby active, desktop wheel-up returned to top, mobile hard wheel landed with intro visible/card 1 active, vertical wheel advanced cards 1->5 with zero window.scrollY delta per step, and horizontal wheel did not change card index. One separate run logged a hydration mismatch in Telegram CTA/HomeCTAButton subtree; another reload run did not reproduce it.
rca of the issue: Observed once in console log: React hydration mismatch error references HomeCTAButton/EditableText subtree, showing server/client attribute/content divergence (caret-color style vs text node). Repro in this iteration was intermittent (seen in run 1, not seen on immediate reload), suggesting state/timing-sensitive SSR-client divergence rather than a deterministic crash.
